hook: You already know how to group things together — your playlist, your contacts, your class schedule. But when mathematicians formalize this idea, surprisingly deep questions emerge: Can every collection of objects be a set? How do you *prove* two collections are equal without listing every element? This unit hands you the precise language that underlies nearly all of modern mathematics.
concept: Every topic here builds on one idea: sets are the fundamental building blocks of mathematical structure, and understanding how they combine, compare, and sometimes break gives you a universal toolkit for reasoning rigorously. Formally, this is the study of *set theory as a foundation for discrete mathematics*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to read and write set notation fluently and compute unions, intersections, differences, and complements to describe exactly which elements belong where.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Subsets, Power Sets, and Cartesian Products
topic_roadmap.description: Master how to enumerate all subsets of a set using the power set $\mathcal{P}(A)$, and build ordered pairs and relations by forming Cartesian products like $A \times B$.
Apply algebraic laws such as De Morgan's laws and the distributive identities to prove set equalities rigorously using element-chasing arguments and logical equivalences.

You will learn to work with infinite collections of sets written as $\bigcup_{i \in I} A_i$ and $\bigcap_{i \in I} A_i$, extending basic operations to powerful indexed notation used throughout advanced mathematics.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Russell's Paradox and Naive Set Theory (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Discover why the intuitive "any collection is a set" assumption leads to a fatal contradiction — Russell's Paradox — and understand why axiomatic set theory was developed to put mathematics on solid ground.
